aliases: the dhabih (the intended sacrifice, in the widely held Islamic identification)
Sadiq al-Wa'd (true to the promise, Q 19:54)

roles: co-builder with Ibrahim of the foundations of the Kaaba
son of the near-sacrifice (the dhabih), in the widely held Islamic identification
summary: In Islam, Isma'il (Ishmael) is a prophet (nabi) and messenger, the son of Ibrahim (Abraham) and Hajar (Hagar), who together with his father raised the foundations of the Kaaba at Mecca and whom Islamic exegesis widely identifies as the son of the near-sacrifice (the dhabih). The Qur'an praises him as true to his promise (Q 19:54) and counts him among the prophets; Islamic tradition associates him with the ancestry of the Arabs.
description: Isma'il is presented here on the terms of the Qur'an and the Islamic tradition. In the Qur'an he is named among the prophets (nabi) and messengers: God reveals to a line of prophets 'and to Ishmael, and Isaac, and Jacob' (Q 4:163, 2:136), and Ishmael is listed with Idris and Dhu al-Kifl among the patient and the righteous (Q 21:85-86). Q 19:54-55 remembers him by name: 'he was true to the promise (sadiq al-waʿd), and he was a messenger, a prophet; and he used to enjoin upon his people prayer and almsgiving, and he was pleasing to his Lord.' He is the son of Ibrahim by Hajar (Hagar), and the Qur'an and its exegesis connect him to the sanctuary at Mecca: with Ibrahim he 'raises the foundations of the House' (the Kaaba) and the two pray that God accept the work and make of their descendants a people submitting to God (Q 2:125-129); Ibrahim's prayer that God settle some of his offspring 'in a valley without cultivation, by Your sacred House' (Q 14:37) is read in the tradition of the settling of Hajar and Ishmael at Mecca, which becomes the setting for the well of Zamzam and the rite of running between al-Safa and al-Marwa. The episode of the near-sacrifice is given in Q 37:99-113: Ibrahim sees in a vision that he is to sacrifice his son, father and son both submit to God's command ('O my father, do as you are commanded; you will find me, God willing, among the steadfast'), and at the point of the act God calls out that the vision has been fulfilled and ransoms the boy with 'a great sacrifice.' The Qur'anic text of this passage does not name the son; classical and later Islamic exegesis widely, though not unanimously in the earliest period, identifies him as Ishmael (Isma'il), the son called the dhabih (the intended sacrifice), and Reuven Firestone has traced how Islamic exegesis moved over time toward this identification. Islamic tradition further associates Ishmael with the ancestry of the Arabs and, through them, with the lineage of the Prophet Muhammad. Across these passages Isma'il stands as a prophet and servant of the one God, a builder of the sanctuary, a model of submission (islam) in the near-sacrifice, and a link in the single line of prophecy (nubuwwa) that the tradition holds runs from Adam through Muhammad.
editorial_notes: This entry follows the per-tradition-pair model of ibrahim (islam) / abraham (judaism) and isa (islam) / jesus-of-nazareth (christianity): a shared figure gets a separate entity per tradition, each on its own terms, joined by a #10 compares_with (here to isaac, judaism). Isma'il is described on Islam's own terms first (neutrality #6, #7): a prophet (nabi) and messenger, son of Ibrahim and Hajar, co-builder of the foundations of the Kaaba, the one 'true to the promise' (sadiq al-waʿd), and, in the widely held identification, the son of the near-sacrifice (the dhabih), with the relevant ayat cited (Q 2:125-129, 4:163, 14:37, 19:54-55, 21:85-86, 37:99-113). The DISPUTED NEAR-SACRIFICE SON is handled per the #10/#11 rule, mirroring abraham/ibrahim: the entry notes accurately that the Qur'anic text of Q 37:99-113 does not name the son, and that classical and later Islamic exegesis widely identifies him as Isma'il, attributed to that exegesis (with Firestone cited on the historical evolution of the identification, which was not unanimous in the earliest period); it does NOT assert as neutral fact which son it 'really' was, and it leaves the Genesis identification (Isaac) to the isaac entry and the compares_with note. NOTE ON NAMES: the Islamic Isma'il corresponds to the biblical Ishmael (Yishmael), a distinct son of Abraham from Isaac (Yitzhak); the two sons must not be conflated, and the compares_with note states plainly that the traditions identify different sons. Sourcing triangulates the three lenses required per #8-9: (a) academic (Reuven Firestone, Journeys in Holy Lands, on the Abraham-Ishmael legends in Islamic exegesis, and Firestone's JAAR article on Abraham's sacrifice for the disputed son), matched to their existing medium rating in ibrahim.json; (b) primary, the Qur'an (the cited ayat) with al-Tabari's classical Sunni tafsir; (c) a contemporary Muslim scholar (Fazlur Rahman, Major Themes of the Qur'an, on prophethood, matched to its existing medium rating in ibrahim.json). No encyclopedias (no Encyclopaedia of Islam), no Wikipedia or tertiary sources. Confidence: the directly cited Qur'anic text is high; the secondary works are medium (verified to exist and be on-topic, not read line-by-line). The compares_with target isaac is authored alongside this entry; related_to targets ibrahim, millat-ibrahim, and nubuwwa exist or are forward references (dangling allowed).

lifespan.note: No birth or death year is asserted. The Islamic tradition places Isma'il among the prophets of a remote past, a son of Ibrahim and long before Muhammad, and the Qur'an is not a chronicle giving his dates; his historical dating is not established, so no CE/BCE integer is given here rather than force a spurious precision. The Qur'anic account of Isma'il belongs to the revelation of 610-632 CE and its classical exegesis.

relations.note: Isma'il (Ishmael) and Isaac (Yitzhak) are not the same person: they are two different sons of Abraham (Ibrahim), and this link compares the shared question that runs across the two traditions, the identity of the son of the binding or near-sacrifice, not the answers, ranking neither and equating neither. Islam holds Isma'il to be a prophet and a son of Ibrahim (and Hajar) who with his father raised the foundations of the Kaaba, and much of Islamic exegesis identifies the son of the near-sacrifice (the dhabih) of Q 37:99-113, a passage that does not name the son, as Isma'il rather than Isaac. Judaism, following Genesis, holds the son whom Abraham binds upon the altar in the Akedah (Genesis 22) to be Isaac, the son of Abraham and Sarah, the second patriarch through whom the covenant line passes to Jacob and Israel. The traditions thus share the question of which son was offered but answer it differently, each identifying a different son; this note records each tradition's identification as attributed to that tradition and does not adjudicate which son it was, rank the two accounts, or harmonize them.

Isma'il (Ishmael) is the son of Ibrahim (Abraham) by Hajar (Hagar), with whom he raises the foundations of the Kaaba (Q 2:127) and who, in the widely held Islamic exegetical identification, is the son of the near-sacrifice (Q 37:99-113); the two are closely linked in the Qur'anic account of the sanctuary and the pilgrimage. This entry is the paired counterpart to ibrahim's related_to reference to isma-il.

As a prophet son of Ibrahim and co-builder of the Kaaba, Isma'il belongs to the primordial upright monotheism the Qur'an calls millat Ibrahim, 'the religion of Abraham' (Q 2:135, 3:95, 16:123); with Ibrahim he prays that God make of their descendants 'a people submitting to You' (Q 2:128).

Isma'il is named among the prophets within the single line of Islamic prophecy (nubuwwa): 'a messenger, a prophet' who enjoined prayer and almsgiving on his people (Q 19:54-55), listed with the other prophets in Q 4:163 and Q 21:85-86.

sources.citation: The Qur'an, esp. Q 2:125-129 (Ibrahim and Ishmael raise the foundations of the House and pray over it), 4:163 (revelation given to Ishmael among the prophets), 14:37 (Ibrahim's prayer settling his offspring by the sacred House), 19:54-55 (Ishmael true to the promise, a messenger and prophet who enjoined prayer and almsgiving), 21:85-86 (Ishmael among the patient and righteous), and 37:99-113 (the near-sacrifice, in which the son is not named and is ransomed with a great sacrifice). Primary scripture, cited on the tradition's own terms.
sources.license: Primary religious text; the classical Arabic is in the public domain; English translations vary.

sources.citation: Firestone, Reuven. Journeys in Holy Lands: The Evolution of the Abraham-Ishmael Legends in Islamic Exegesis. Albany: State University of New York Press, 1990. An academic monograph presenting and analysing the Islamic legends about Abraham and Ishmael from the Qur'an and early exegesis (the journeys, the settling of Hajar and Ishmael at Mecca, the building of the Kaaba, and the sacrifice, including the question of Isaac or Ishmael as the intended son) in relation to Jewish, Christian, and pre-Islamic Arabian traditions. Cited as the academic lens on the Islamic Abraham-Ishmael material; matched to its existing medium rating in ibrahim.json. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Firestone, Reuven. 'Merit, Mimesis, and Martyrdom: Aspects of Shiʿite Meta-Historical Exegesis on Abraham's Sacrifice in Light of Jewish, Christian, and Sunni Muslim Tradition.' Journal of the American Academy of Religion 66, no. 1 (1998): 93-116. A peer-reviewed article on the exegesis of Abraham's near-sacrifice across traditions, bearing on the question of the intended son (the dhabih) and the movement of Islamic exegesis toward the identification of Ishmael. Cited specifically for the disputed identity of the son of the sacrifice; matched to its existing medium rating in ibrahim.json. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary journal article; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: al-Tabari, Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Jarir. Jami' al-bayan 'an ta'wil ay al-Qur'an (The Comprehensive Exposition of the Interpretation of the Verses of the Qur'an), completed c. 883 CE. Classical Sunni tafsir; the foundational classical commentary on the Qur'anic passages on Isma'il, including Q 37:99-113 on the near-sacrifice, where the exegetical tradition debates the identity of the son (Isaac or Ishmael); al-Tabari himself weighs the competing reports and favors Isaac, which concretely shows that the Ishmael identification, though it became the widely held view, was not unanimous. Cited as the classical primary-exegesis lens; the Arabic passages were not read line-by-line in this pass.
sources.license: Primary classical text; Arabic in the public domain.

sources.citation: Rahman, Fazlur. Major Themes of the Qur'ān. 2nd ed. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 2009 (1st ed. Minneapolis: Bibliotheca Islamica, 1980). A monograph by a leading modern Muslim scholar; its chapter on prophethood and revelation treats the Qur'anic prophets, among them Ishmael, and the Qur'anic account of prophetic submission to God. Cited as the contemporary Muslim scholarly lens; matched to its existing medium rating in ibrahim.json. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.
